narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 43% chance that you might fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is above our threshold for approval. This prediction is based on your overall profile, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.20, which is right around the average for applicants; your notably low LSAT score of 19, far below the typical 36.7; your school decile rankings of 7th in Year 1 and 8th in Year 3, both higher—and thus indicating slightly weaker performance—than the averages of about 5.7 and 5.5; your low family income bracket; your status as an Asian female planning full-time studies; and the fact that full-time enrollment is the norm for most applicants. When the model weighed these factors, your Year 3 decile ranking had the strongest influence, pushing the prediction toward a higher risk of bar failure because it's in the lower half of your school's performance distribution. Right behind that was your Year 1 decile, which similarly nudged the odds toward failure compared to stronger performers. Your low family income came next in influence, also contributing toward the failure prediction, as it often correlates with additional challenges in this dataset. Your LSAT score followed closely, strongly signaling risk due to how far below average it is. Your racial background as Asian had a moderate pull in the same direction, given its underrepresentation relative to the mostly White applicant pool. Your undergraduate GPA added a smaller push toward failure, being just a touch below par. In a positive note, your gender as female was the one factor that worked in your favor, slightly lowering the predicted risk compared to the male majority. Your full-time status had the least impact, barely shifting the needle since it's so common. Overall, these stacked up to tip the scales against you, even though your UGPA was solid and your gender helped a bit. To improve your chances next time, focus on what you can control: retake the LSAT to boost that critical score closer to or above average, strengthen your academic deciles through better grades if reapplying after more school years, and perhaps seek scholarships or support to offset family income barriers. With targeted prep, especially on LSAT and consistent academic performance, you could shift this prediction favorably and open doors to admission. Keep pushing—you've got strengths like your average GPA and determination that shine through.
